path.title: The Science of Well-Being
path.subtitle: Build happiness with evidence, not guesswork
path.summary: Most people chase happiness through instinct and culture, and get predictable but wrong results. This path shows you how psychology actually explains happiness, and how to apply it in daily life. You walk away able to diagnose your own well-being and implement habits that measurably improve it.

skills.name: Cognitive Bias Awareness
skills.description: Recognize systematic thinking errors that distort happiness judgments

skills.name: Hedonic Adaptation Understanding
skills.description: Understand how humans quickly normalize positive and negative changes

skills.name: Behavioral Rewiring
skills.description: Design habits that change behavior beyond knowledge

skills.name: Gratitude Practice
skills.description: Use structured gratitude techniques to increase well-being

skills.name: Social Connection Design
skills.description: Intentionally build meaningful relationships for well-being

skills.name: Well-Being Diagnostics
skills.description: Assess and track your happiness using structured methods

lessons.title: Why You Want the Wrong Things
lessons.subtitle: Miswanting drives most life decisions

blocks.md: You think you know what will make you happy. You are usually wrong. The mistake is not random; it follows patterns. This lesson overturns the idea that desire equals value.

blocks.title: Mental model
blocks.md: Think of your brain as a prediction engine trained on biased data. It predicts happiness poorly because it overweights vivid outcomes and ignores adaptation.
Example: You expect a ₹20 lakh salary jump to increase happiness permanently. In reality, satisfaction spikes for 2–3 months, then resets.

choices.label: Wanting things that do not increase long-term happiness
choices.explain: Correct; this is the core idea.

choices.label: Wanting fewer things
choices.explain: Tempting but incorrect; miswanting is about accuracy, not quantity.

choices.label: Not wanting anything
choices.explain: Did not read carefully; this is not about desire elimination.

blocks.md: Data shows happiness returns to baseline within months.

blocks.title: The classic trap
blocks.md: Knowing this does not change behavior. This is the G.I. Joe Fallacy.

blocks.instruction: List 3 things you believe will make you happy, then write how long the effect will realistically last.
blocks.expected: Example: New job; 3 months spike, then baseline. New phone; 2 weeks excitement. Travel; 1 month residual happiness.

points: Your brain predicts happiness poorly in systematic ways
Big life changes create temporary spikes, not permanent shifts
Miswanting leads to repeated poor decisions

blocks.prompt: What is one goal you are pursuing that may be driven by miswanting?

blocks.md: You assume good things will keep feeling good. They do not. Your brain normalizes everything.

blocks.title: Mental model
blocks.md: Think of happiness like a thermostat set at 7 out of 10. External changes push it briefly, then it returns.
Example: Buying a ₹1 lakh phone increases happiness for 14 days; then it becomes normal.

choices.label: Happiness returns to baseline after changes
Only bad events fade away
choices.explain: Did not read carefully; good events fade too.

blocks.md: Example: Instead of eating at the same restaurant weekly, rotate across 5 places; this slows adaptation.

blocks.title: The classic trap
blocks.md: You try to outrun adaptation by increasing intensity, not variety.

blocks.prompt: Order the steps to reduce adaptation

points: Variety beats intensity for sustained happiness

blocks.prompt: Where in your life are you chasing intensity instead of variety?

assessment.title: The Science of Well-Being Assessment
assessment.description: Tests your ability to identify biases and apply well-being strategies

choices.label: Believing happiness comes after achieving a goal

project.title: Capstone: Design Your Happiness System
project.prompt: Your friend says: 'I have a good job but still feel unhappy. What should I do?'' Design a 14-day plan using course principles. Ask clarifying questions, identify biases, and propose interventions.

credential.title: The Science of Well-Being
credential.description: Can apply psychological science to improve personal happiness and well-being
